scenarios.task: We set our whole summer season's staffing schedule back in the spring based on typical historical attendance patterns, and we're sticking with that plan throughout the season regardless of week-to-week weather forecasts or local event calendars.
criteria: Flags relying solely on the pre-set seasonal schedule as the static-seasonal-staffing failure mode
States that this produces chronic overstaffing on slow days and understaffing on peak days without a rolling short-term forecast adjustment
Recommends building staffing from a rolling short-term forecast (weather, pre-sale data, local events) refreshed close to the operating date
Doesn't reject having a baseline seasonal plan — the issue is never adjusting it against short-term signals

scenarios.task: Our rain-day contingency plan (indoor programming, rebooking policy) is the same regardless of which day of the season it is — we apply the identical plan to a slow weekday in the off-season and to our highest-revenue holiday weekend.
criteria: Flags applying uniform contingency capacity across the calendar as the even-contingency-investment-across-the-calendar failure mode
States that a bad-weather day during peak season represents a disproportionate share of annual revenue loss compared to the same weather in the off-season
Recommends sizing contingency investment disproportionately to each specific window's revenue concentration, not spreading it evenly
Frames peak-window contingency as having outsized value specifically, not as a uniform baseline requirement

scenarios.task: We're implementing virtual queuing/timed entry across every single attraction in the park uniformly, since it seems like the fairest and most consistent approach for all guests regardless of which ride they're visiting.
criteria: Flags uniform queue management across all attractions as the uniform-queue-management failure mode
States that the guest-experience and secondary-spend payoff concentrates specifically on attractions where wait time is actually the binding constraint on satisfaction
Recommends applying capacity-managed queuing specifically to the highest-demand attractions rather than uniformly everywhere
Notes that applying it everywhere (or nowhere) misses where the actual value concentrates

scenarios.task: We're short on labor budget this season, so we're compressing lifeguard and ride-operator training time and reducing supervisory coverage on the water attractions to save on labor cost, treating our large seasonal workforce as largely interchangeable.
criteria: Flags compressing training and supervision on physically risky activities to save labor cost as the treating-seasonal-staff-as-interchangeable failure mode
States that undertraining or under-supervising creates a real safety risk disguised as a staffing efficiency, not just a service-quality tradeoff
Recommends treating training depth and supervision ratios on safety-relevant activities as a safety control, not a labor-cost line to cut
Frames this the same category error as compromising a hard operating limit under demand pressure
